findings.description: flush() calls _pending.clear() unconditionally after the send loop; if any sendmail() call raises, all remaining unsent messages are silently discarded with no recovery path and no indication in the return value.

smtplib.SMTP() is called outside the try block; if the constructor raises (e.g., connection refused), the exception propagates out of the for-loop immediately, all remaining messages are skipped, and _pending.clear() on line 65 still destroys them.

Dedup key is msg.to alone; two semantically distinct messages to the same recipient (e.g., password-reset and welcome email) will cause the second to be silently dropped and counted as skipped, which is almost certainly wrong for a general-purpose queue.

clear_dedup_cache() clears _seen but not _pending; a caller who calls this to allow re-enqueue of an address will find the original message still queued, leading to duplicate sends when flush() is called.

Header injection risk: _render() builds raw SMTP headers by f-string interpolation of msg.subject and msg.to without sanitizing embedded CRLF sequences, allowing an attacker-controlled subject or recipient to inject arbitrary headers.

preview() uses bare LF line endings while _render() uses CRLF; the two representations of the same message are inconsistent, which will cause diverging behaviour if callers use preview() output as a proxy for the wire format.
MIMEText is imported but never used; _render() builds a raw string instead, making the import dead code and signalling an incomplete or abandoned refactor toward proper MIME construction.

A new SMTP connection is opened and torn down for every message inside the flush loop; for any non-trivial queue size this is dramatically inefficient compared to a single connection reused for all messages in the batch.
